title: monitoring — model relationships
description: Six owned entities. HealthCheck is the parent of HealthCheckRun. MonitoringAlertPolicy is the parent of MonitoringAlert. MonitoringIncident groups MonitoringAlert rows (nullable — an alert may not yet be grouped). MonitoringProviderConfig is standalone (tenant-scoped) — alerts reference providers via the policy's notify_provider_configs jsonb array, not a direct FK. Every outbound FK stays same-tenant per tenancy-columns.md §5. NO application_id / region_id / organization_id / scope_node_id on any owned row.

internal.description: Per-execution runs. RESTRICT on health_check hard-delete — runs anchor to their check for audit.
RESTRICT — runs anchor to their check. Check's hard-delete only happens after 90-day grace + all its runs are purged.
Every alert fires against a policy. RESTRICT on policy delete.
RESTRICT — alerts anchor to their firing policy for auditing.
Grouped alerts. Nullable — an alert may not yet be grouped.
SET NULL on incident hard-delete — a hard-deleted incident releases its alerts back to un-grouped state (unusual — usually the incident lives longer than the alerts).

outbound.description: Denormalised tenant_id — cascades through health_check.tenant_id but explicit for query-plan + FK integrity.
The user who acknowledged the alert. RESTRICT for audit-integrity — user hard-delete cannot orphan acknowledgment records.
The user who manually resolved (NULL for auto-resolve).
The user who acknowledged the incident.
The user who manually resolved (NULL for auto-resolve).

inbound.description: Monitoring does NOT have inbound relations. No downstream module attaches to monitoring rows. Monitoring is a leaf in the observability tier — its role is to observe the system, not to be consumed by other domain rows.
inbound.note: The audit + activity modules read monitoring events for compliance retention but do NOT hold FKs on monitoring rows. Their tables ({audits, activity_log}) store the audit trail with tenant_id + application_id (per tenancy-columns.md §2) — those two rows DO carry application_id; the six monitoring rows do NOT.

notes: None of the six owned rows carry `application_id` — cascades through tenant_id → tenants.application_id per tenancy-columns.md §2. Only the 8 named rows carry application_id directly; monitoring_* is not among them.
None of the six owned rows carry `region_id`, `organization_id`, or `scope_node_id`. Monitoring is a domain-plane concern — the growth/scope substrate does not apply.

MonitoringAlertPolicy.notify_provider_configs is a jsonb array of MonitoringProviderConfig ULIDs — NOT a FK. This is deliberate — the array is deep-copied at policy save + the individual configs are RESTRICT on delete. This lets a config be de-configured from a policy without the delete cascade rewriting the policy's arrays.
MonitoringIncident.oncall_page_config is similarly a jsonb — { primary_provider: <config_id>, escalation_policy: { after_seconds, escalation_provider: <config_id> } }. NOT FKs.

The monitoring_alert.monitoring_incident_id nullable FK is the ONLY optional attachment — every alert eventually attaches to an incident (via GroupAlertsIntoIncidentJob) but the attachment is asynchronous. Alerts in status='suppressed' or 'expired' may never attach — a nullable FK is correct.
